metadata.summary: Linear issue tracking: cycles with Fibonacci points, triage workflow, projects and roadmaps, GitHub integration with auto-transitions, and custom views for team focus.

content.title: Linear for High-Velocity Software Teams
content.summary: Linear is purpose-built for high-velocity software teams. Its keyboard-first interface (C create, S status, P priority) reduces task management overhead to seconds per issue. The "Linear Method" — cycles for time-boxing, projects for initiatives, roadmap for long-term vision — provides structure without configuration overhead. GitHub integration with "Fixes BAK-NNN" auto-transitions issues through In Review and Done without manual updates. Linear's GraphQL API supports full CRUD operations and webhooks for agent-driven automation.

why.p: Linear is purpose-built for high-velocity software teams. Its keyboard-first interface (C create, S status, P priority) reduces task management overhead to seconds per issue. The "Linear Method" — cycles for time-boxing, projects for initiatives, roadmap for long-term vision — provides structure without configuration overhead.

GitHub integration with "Fixes BAK-NNN" auto-transitions issues through In Review and Done without manual updates. Linear's opinionated workflow prioritizes speed: minimal configuration and automatic cycle management over flexibility.

list.item: Engineering team building software products needs fast, low-friction issue tracking.
Team values keyboard-driven workflows and wants to minimize PM tool overhead.
Startup or growth-stage company needing minimal configuration to start.
Continuous deployment team where issues move through states quickly.
GitHub is the code host — Linear's GitHub integration is best-in-class.
Non-technical stakeholders need to update issues — Linear's UI has a learning curve compared to Trello or Notion.
OKR or goal tracking alongside issue tracking — Linear has limited goal management; use ClickUp.
Complex documentation alongside tasks — Linear has no docs; use Notion.
Detailed time tracking or billing required — Linear has no native time tracking.

section.name: team-configuration
section.p: Linear's team configuration establishes the foundation for velocity tracking, cycle planning, and agent automation.

rule.statement: Create one Linear Team per product area (e.g., Backend, Frontend, DevOps). Each team gets a short key prefix (BAK, FRD, OPS) that appears in issue IDs (BAK-123). Use Fibonacci point scale [0, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13] for estimates — do not customize to a linear scale.
rule.rationale: Fibonacci scale encodes that estimation uncertainty grows with issue size. Linear scales create false precision for large issues.

rule.statement: Configure cycle duration to 2 weeks starting on Monday with auto-archive enabled. Create 2 upcoming cycles in advance so sprint planning can pull from the backlog into future cycles without manual cycle creation.
rule.rationale: Auto-archive on cycle end moves completed issues out of the active view automatically. Pre-creating upcoming cycles enables planning ahead without blocking the current cycle.

rule.statement: Enable Triage with no default assignee. All new issues enter Triage before being moved to the team backlog. Triage is a review gate, not a workflow status.
rule.rationale: Without triage, unscoped issues pollute the backlog and distort cycle planning. The triage step enforces minimum issue quality before any work is committed.

section.name: triage-workflow
section.p: A consistent triage workflow prevents issues from stagnating and ensures only scoped work enters the backlog.

rule.statement: Apply the triage decision tree in order: Is it valid? No → Close or mark Duplicate. Is it urgent (P0/P1 bug)? Yes → assign to current cycle with High priority immediately. Is it scoped? No → add "Needs Refinement" label and leave in Triage. Yes → move to Backlog with appropriate labels and estimate.
rule.rationale: A consistent triage decision tree prevents issues from stagnating in Triage or bypassing refinement before entering the backlog.

section.name: issue-properties
section.p: Every cycle commitment requires consistent metadata to support velocity calculations and agent triage.

rule.statement: Every issue committed to a cycle must have: Status set, Priority set, Estimate (Fibonacci points), Assignee, and at least one acceptance criterion in the description. Issues without estimates in a cycle cause velocity calculations to be meaningless.
rule.rationale: Unestimated cycle issues skew velocity metrics — you cannot calculate planned vs. completed points without estimates on all committed issues.

rule.statement: Use sub-issues for issues larger than 8 points. An issue with 13+ points is a project-level item that should be broken down into 2-5 sub-issues of 1-5 points each. Assign the parent issue as the project; sub-issues are the cycle work items.
rule.rationale: Large issues that span multiple cycles create incomplete carry-over. Breaking them down enables partial delivery and more accurate cycle planning.

section.name: projects-and-roadmap
section.p: Linear projects and roadmaps provide long-term scope tracking beyond the cycle time horizon.

rule.statement: Use Projects for multi-cycle initiatives (e.g., "User Authentication Overhaul"). Projects span cycles and track progress across issues. Cycles provide time-boxing within the initiative. Never use cycles as a substitute for projects — a cycle is a time box, a project is a scope.
rule.rationale: Conflating projects and cycles creates confusion: "Sprint 24" and "OAuth Feature" are different abstractions. Projects group related work by feature; cycles group work by time period.

rule.statement: Add milestones within projects for key deliverables. Each milestone has a target date and a list of issues that must be Done before the milestone is met. Milestones appear on the roadmap view.
rule.rationale: Milestones provide stakeholder-visible checkpoints without requiring the entire project to be complete. They enable partial delivery communication.

section.name: github-integration
section.p: Linear's GitHub integration provides code-task traceability without manual updates.

rule.statement: Configure branch naming pattern: "{username}/{team-prefix}-{issue_number}-{issue_title_slug}". This enables Linear to automatically link branches to issues without manual linking.
rule.rationale: Automatic branch-to-issue linking is the foundation of the code-task traceability workflow. Manual linking is forgotten by developers under deadline pressure.

rule.statement: Enable PR status sync: pr_opened → "In Review", pr_merged → "Done". Use "Fixes BAK-123" or "Closes BAK-123" in PR descriptions — not "Relates to". Only "Fixes/Closes/Resolves" trigger automatic transitions; "Relates to" does not.
rule.rationale: The keyword matters: "Relates to BAK-123" creates a link but does not transition the issue. Teams using the wrong keyword lose the auto-close workflow.

section.name: custom-views
section.p: Purpose-specific views reduce cognitive load and surface the right information for each workflow.

rule.statement: Create these standard views: "My Work" (filter: assignee=me, status!=done, group by priority), "Team Board" (filter: team=backend, group by status, show estimates), "Bug Triage" (filter: label=bug, status=triage, sort by created), "Release Scope" (filter: project=vX.Y, status!=canceled, group by status). Each developer should mark "My Work" as their default view.
rule.rationale: The default view determines what a developer sees when they open Linear. "My Work" reduces noise by showing only their assigned items.

section.name: cycle-management
section.p: Cycle governance ensures velocity predictability and prevents scope creep.

rule.statement: Freeze cycle scope after Day 2 of the cycle. Scope additions after Day 2 require removing an existing issue of equal or greater points. Track mid-cycle scope additions in the retrospective.
rule.rationale: Mid-cycle scope additions are the primary cause of cycle incompleteness. A points-in/points-out rule maintains commitments without preventing urgent fixes.

rule.statement: At cycle end, review all incomplete issues before moving them to the next cycle. Incomplete issues that are still valid go to the next cycle. Issues that became invalid or were superseded go back to the Backlog or are canceled. Never auto-move all incomplete issues forward without review.
rule.rationale: Auto-forwarding incomplete issues without review creates a cycle-over-cycle backlog of stale work that inflates planned velocity without delivering value.

antipattern.description: Automatically moving all incomplete issues from Cycle N to Cycle N+1 after cycle close.
antipattern.reason: Stale issues accumulate in active cycles, inflating planned velocity. Review filters out issues that are no longer relevant before committing them to the next cycle.

section.name: agent-integration
section.p: Linear's GraphQL API and webhooks enable full cycle automation.

rule.statement: Use Linear's GraphQL complexity limit (~1000 complexity units per query) as a constraint. Deeply nested queries with many relations hit limits and return partial results. Paginate with first: 50 and after: cursor for large datasets.
rule.rationale: Over-fetching consumes complexity budget and slows responses; pagination ensures bounded query cost.

rule.statement: Webhooks are signed with HMAC-SHA256 — agents must verify the signature or accept spoofed events. Do not skip signature validation.
rule.rationale: Webhook signature verification is essential for security when receiving state-changing events.

rule.statement: The assignee field returns null for unassigned issues, not an empty string — agents must handle null explicitly.
rule.rationale: Type safety requires checking for null rather than empty strings.

rule.statement: Cycle auto-archive: when a cycle ends, Linear auto-archives incomplete issues unless configured otherwise. Agents that read "current cycle" may get zero results if the cycle just closed.
rule.rationale: Timing matters when querying active cycles; archived cycles are excluded from the active cycle query.

rule.statement: Priority is an integer (0=No priority, 1=Urgent, 2=High, 3=Medium, 4=Low) in the API, not a string — agents that output strings must map back to integers before writing.
rule.rationale: Type conversions are necessary when transforming between API and display representations.

rule.statement: Require human checkpoint before bulk issue reassignment or bulk cycle changes — Linear has no undo for API-driven bulk mutations.
rule.rationale: Bulk API operations are irreversible; human review prevents cascading errors.
